{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.4}}

Revised draft below, followed by the main issues that drove the changes.

---

**Revised draft**

We present SelectConsensus, a distributed consensus protocol for heterogeneous multi-agent control systems. At each timestep, every agent proposes a candidate control action and a designated leader *selects* one — rather than averaging across proposals — based on a one-step predicted residual under a shared linearized dynamics model. The motivating intuition is that when agents differ substantially in sensor quality or local model fidelity, the best individual proposal carries more useful information than any convex combination, since averaging dilutes the strongest candidate with weaker ones.

We evaluate SelectConsensus against SimpleAverage, our prior averaging-based protocol (Author et al., 2023), on three benchmarks chosen to span aerial, electrical, and ground-vehicle domains:

- Cooperative formation control of a 6-quadrotor swarm: RMS tracking error drops from 0.34 m to 0.21 m (−38%).
- Decentralized voltage regulation on a 9-bus microgrid: bus-voltage deviation drops from 2.7% to 1.9% (−30%).
- Coordinated lane-keeping in a 4-vehicle platoon: mean headway error drops from 0.18 m to 0.12 m (−33%).

An ablation that disables selection and reverts to averaging recovers SimpleAverage's numbers on all three tasks, isolating the aggregation rule as the source of the gain. The selection rule and its residual predictor are defined in Section 4; the ablation appears in Section 5.

SelectConsensus is intended as a drop-in replacement for averaging in existing consensus stacks: it preserves the decentralized communication topology of SimpleAverage and changes only the aggregation block (fewer than 80 lines in our ROS2 reference implementation). Two caveats temper the claim of strict improvement. First, our gains are measured against a single averaging baseline on three tasks; whether the advantage holds against richer aggregators (weighted averaging with covariance estimates, median consensus, trimmed mean) is left to future work. Second, the leader-based selection introduces a single point of failure that pure averaging does not have; we discuss leader-failover overhead in Section 6.

---

**Main issues addressed**

1. *Overclaimed framing.* The original closes with "strict improvement over averaging-based consensus" and "new design space," but the evidence is one prior protocol on three tasks. I softened the contribution to "drop-in replacement" and added explicit caveats about untested baselines (weighted/robust aggregators) and the leader single-point-of-failure tradeoff. The phrase "since its inception" was also unsupported puffery and is gone.

2. *Comparison set is thin.* SimpleAverage is the only baseline named, and it's the authors' own prior work. The revised draft makes that explicit rather than letting it pass as "averaging-based consensus" in general. The literature on consensus has long included weighted, median, and trimmed-mean variants — the abstract should not imply these were beaten.

3. *Evidence-claim mismatch on the ablation.* The original says the ablation "degrades all three metrics by roughly the margin reported above." If disabling selection literally *is* SimpleAverage, the degradation should match exactly, not roughly. Either the ablation isn't quite SimpleAverage (in which case the framing as a clean isolation is misleading) or "roughly" is a hedge that shouldn't be there. I rewrote it to assert exact recovery; if that's not what the data show, the authors should explain the residual gap.

4. *Structure of the results paragraph.* The three benchmarks were buried in prose. Bulleting them with percent deltas makes the magnitude of the improvement immediately legible and makes the cross-domain claim (aerial/electrical/ground) explicit rather than implicit.

5. *Redundant restatement of the contribution.* The original opens *and* closes with the selection-vs-averaging contrast, and Section 4's intuition is also previewed in the opening. I consolidated the mechanism, intuition, and section pointer into one place so the abstract isn't circling the same point three times.

6. *Self-citation hygiene.* "Author et al., 2023" is a placeholder; the final version needs the real citation and, ideally, one or two non-self citations situating SimpleAverage within the broader averaging-consensus literature (Olfati-Saber, Ren & Beard, etc.) so the comparison has external grounding.

7. *Sentence-level cleanup.* "selects, rather than averages, the chosen action" was self-contradictory ("selects … the chosen action"); "we believe lowers the bar for adoption in operational fleets" is unsupported editorializing and was cut.

---

**Uncertainty disclosure.** Recommendation 3 (the ablation should exactly recover SimpleAverage's numbers) assumes the only difference between the two protocols is the aggregation rule; if SelectConsensus also changes the residual model, leader-election cadence, or message schedule, "roughly" may be correct and my rewrite overclaims. Recommendation 6's specific citation suggestions are from general knowledge of the consensus-control literature and were not verified against the authors' actual reference list.
